title: Ensure WorkSpaces are deployed in their own virtual private cloud (VPC)

input_row.title: Ensure WorkSpaces are deployed in their own virtual private cloud (VPC)
input_row.description: Amazon WorkSpaces VPC should be created with two private subnets for your WorkSpaces and a NAT gateway in a public subnet.
input_row.rationale: The NAT gateway will provide WorkSpaces access to the internet for updates to the operating system and so that applications can be deployed using Amazon WorkSpaces Application Manager if that is applicable for your environment.
input_row.audit: Perform the following steps to confirm that a VPC exists for WorkSpaces and is configured correctly. From the Console: 1. Login to the VPC console at https://console.aws.amazon.com/vpc/ 2. In the left pane, click Your VPC's 3. Select the VPC for WorkSpaces 4. Confirm the IPv4 settings are using a CIDR block from the private (non-publicly routable) IP address ranges. For example, 10.0.0.0/16. For more information, see the references below. 5. Confirm the IPv6 CIDR Block, set to No. 6. Confirm the IPv4 CIDR block for the public subnet (example - WorkSpaces Public Subnet) • Availability Zone, set to No Preference. 7. Confirm the IPv4 CIDR block for the first private subnet (example - WorkSpaces Private Subnet 1) - Availability Zone, set for Amazon WorkSpaces. - Elastic IP Allocation ID - Service endpoints - `Blank` - Enable DNS hostnames, set to `Yes`. - Hardware tenancy, Default. 8. Confirm the IPv4 CIDR block for the first private subnet (example - WorkSpaces Private Subnet 2) - Availability Zone set for Amazon WorkSpaces. NOTE-Make sure you select a different Availability Zone from the one you selected for the Workspaces Private Subnet 1 - Elastic IP Allocation ID - Service endpoints - Blank - Enable DNS hostnames, set to `Yes`. - Hardware tenancy, Default. If this is not set as referenced above refer to the remediation procedure below.
input_row.remediation: Perform the following steps to create a VPC for Workspaces From the Console: Allocate an Elastic IP Address 1. Login in to the VPC console at https://console.aws.amazon.com/vpc/ 2. In the left pane, click Elastic IPs. 3. Click Allocate new address. 4. On the Allocate new address page, for iPv4 address pool, click Amazon pool or Owned by me 5. Click Allocate. 6. Make a note of the Elastic IP address, click Close. Create a VPC with one public subnet and two private subnets as follows. 1. Login in to the VPC console at https://console.aws.amazon.com/vpc/ 2. In the left pane, click VPC Dashboard in the upper-left corner. 3. Click Launch VPC Wizard. 4. Click VPC with Public and Private Subnets 5. Click Select. 6. Configure the VPC as follows: - For IPv4 CIDR block, enter the CIDR block from the private (non-publicly routable) IP address ranges. - example 10.0.0.0/16. - For IPv6 CIDR Block, keep `No IPv6 CIDR Block`. - For VPC name, enter a `name for the VPC` (example: WorkspacesVPC) - Public subnet's IPv4 CIDR - enter a CIDR block from the private (non-publicly routable) IP address range - ie. 10.0.0.0/24. - For Availability Zone, keep `No Preference`. - For Public subnet name, enter a `name for the subnet` (example: WorkSpaces Public Subnet). - For Private subnet's IPv4 CIDR, enter the CIDR block for the subnet. - `Availability Zone` - Accept the default value - No Preference - For Private subnet name, enter a `name for the subnet` (example: WorkSpaces Private Subnet 1). - For Elastic IP Allocation ID, enter the Elastic IP address that you created. - For Service endpoints, `do nothing`. - For Enable DNS hostnames, keep `Yes`. - For Hardware tenancy, keep `Default`. 7. Click Create VPC. Note that it takes several minutes to set up your VPC. After the VPC is created. 8. Click OK. Create a Second Private Subnet 1. In the left pane, click Subnets. 2. Click Create Subnet. - For Name tag, enter a `name for the private subnet` (example: WorkSpaces Private Subnet 2). - For VPC, `select the VPC` that you created. - For Availability Zone. Make sure you select a different Availability Zone from the one used in WorkSpaces Private Subnet 1. - For IPv4 CIDR block, enter the CIDR block for the subnet. 3. Click Create. Verify and Name the Route Tables for Public 1. In the left pane, click Subnets 2. Click the public subnet that you created. (example: WorkSpaces Public Subnet) 3. On the Route Table tab, choose the ID of the route table (example: rtb- 12345678). 4. Click the route table. 5. Under Name, choose the edit icon, enter a name (example: workspaces-public- routetable) 6. Click the check mark to save the name. 7. On the Routes tab, verify that there is one route for local traffic and another route that sends all other traffic to the internet gateway for the VPC. Verify and Name the Route Tables for Private 1. In the left pane, click Subnets 2. Click the private subnet 1 that you created. (example: WorkSpaces Private Subnet 1) 3. On the Route Table tab, choose the ID of the route table (example: rtb- 12345678). 4. Click the route table. 5. Under Name, choose the edit icon, enter a name (example: workspaces-private- routetable) 6. Click the check mark to save the name. 7. On the Routes tab, verify that there is one route for local traffic and another route that sends all other traffic to the NAT gateway. 8. Repeat steps 1-7 under Verify and Name the Route Tables for Private' for WorkSpaces Private Subnet 2`
input_row.impact: Your VPC's subnets must be in different Availability Zones in the Region where you're launching WorkSpaces.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
- The audit control specifies a requirement for the Amazon WorkSpaces VPC configuration, which involves certain properties of the VPC and subnets that can be determined via AWS APIs.
- The checks can be made against the VPC configuration, confirming the existence of the necessary subnets (private and public), and their respective CIDR blocks as specified in the control. Boto3 provides functions to access this information efficiently.
- Standard AWS API calls exist for checking VPC and Subnet settings. The criteria set forth in the control can be verified without human interpretation or physical access.

**PROGRAM NAME:** aws_vpc_workspaces_configuration_compliant

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- Use the following AWS API calls or boto3 methods to verify the configuration:

1. **Retrieve VPC Information:**
   ```python
   import boto3

   ec2 = boto3.client('ec2')
   vpcs = ec2.describe_vpcs()
   ```
   - **Check for WorkSpaces-specific VPC:** Verify if the VPC has two private subnets and one public subnet.

2. **Get Subnet Information:**
   ```python
   subnets = ec2.describe_subnets()
   ```
   - **Validation of Subnet CIDR Blocks:**
     - Confirm there’s one public subnet and two private subnets.
     - Ensure the public subnet's CIDR block is in the range of private non-publicly routable IP addresses.
     - Ensure the first private subnet and the second private subnet’s CIDR blocks are in the appropriate ranges and that they are in different Availability Zones within the same region.

3. **Check NAT Gateway:**
   ```python
   nat_gateways = ec2.describe_nat_gateways()
   ```
   - **Validation**: Ensure that a NAT Gateway exists in the public subnet configuration to facilitate internet access for WorkSpaces.

4. **Confirm DNS Hostnames:**
   For each subnet, check the `EnableDnsHostnames` property:
   ```python
   for subnet in subnets['Subnets']:
       if subnet['VpcId'] == 'specific_vpc_id':  # Replace with actual VPC ID
           assert subnet['EnableDnsHostnames'] == True, "DNS hostnames not enabled for subnet."
   ```

5. **Pass/Fail Criteria:**
   - **Pass if:**
     - The configuration includes 1 public and 2 private subnets.
     - Correct CIDR blocks are assigned to the public and private subnets.
     - All private subnets have `EnableDnsHostnames` set to `Yes`.
     - A NAT Gateway exists for internet access.

   - **Fail if:**
     - The required subnets do not exist or are improperly configured.
     - Subnets are in the same Availability Zone.
     - The NAT Gateway is absent or incorrectly set up.

By validating these conditions programmatically, the audit can confirm compliance with the specified control within the AWS VPC setup for Amazon WorkSpaces.
step2_review:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** AGREE

- The classification of this audit as Automated is correct. The compliance check can be effectively performed using AWS APIs, specifically through boto3 or AWS CLI, without the need for manual intervention. AWS SDK capabilities are sufficient for retrieving and verifying VPC and subnet configurations, as well as NAT Gateway existence and configuration.

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 7

- The automation details are mostly accurate, but there are some areas that need improvement:
  - The code snippets provided do not include filtering logic to specifically identify the VPC associated with Amazon WorkSpaces. This could lead to incorrect results if multiple VPCs are present.
  - The `EnableDnsHostnames` property is a VPC-level attribute, not a subnet-level attribute. The code snippet incorrectly attempts to check this at the subnet level.
  - The program name `aws_vpc_workspaces_configuration_compliant` is somewhat descriptive but could be more specific about the checks being performed.

**IMPROVEMENTS:**

- **Filtering Logic:** Add filters to the `describe_vpcs` and `describe_subnets` calls to ensure that the checks are performed on the correct VPC and subnets associated with Amazon WorkSpaces.
  ```python
  vpcs = ec2.describe_vpcs(Filters=[{'Name': 'tag:Name', 'Values': ['WorkSpacesVPC']}])
  subnets = ec2.describe_subnets(Filters=[{'Name': 'vpc-id', 'Values': [vpc_id]}])
  ```
- **Correct DNS Hostnames Check:** The `EnableDnsHostnames` property should be checked at the VPC level, not the subnet level.
  ```python
  vpc_attributes = ec2.describe_vpc_attribute(VpcId='vpc_id', Attribute='enableDnsHostnames')
  assert vpc_attributes['EnableDnsHostnames']['Value'] == True, "DNS hostnames not enabled for VPC."
  ```
- **Program Name Suggestion:** Consider renaming the program to `aws_vpc_workspaces_vpc_structure_compliance` to better reflect the specific checks being performed.

**CONFIDENCE:** MEDIUM

- While the general approach and use of AWS APIs are correct, the technical inaccuracies and lack of filtering logic reduce confidence in the assessment's completeness and precision.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:** Modify

- Modify the automation details to include filtering logic for identifying the correct VPC and subnets associated with Amazon WorkSpaces.
- Correct the check for `EnableDnsHostnames` to be at the VPC level.
- Consider renaming the program for better clarity and specificity.
- These modifications will enhance the accuracy and reliability of the automated compliance check.
